sections.title: Core Charter
sections.preview: DDL identity, mission, vision, and the 8-step build sequence: Gather → Sort → Structure → Encode → Govern → Automate → Beautify → Preserve.
sections.content:
DDL Core Charter

FILE: DDL_CoreCharter_v1.0.txt
TITLE: Dropdown Logistics — Core Charter

1. IDENTITY

Name: Dropdown Logistics (DDL)
Tagline: “From scattered to structured.”

DDL is your personal “ops studio” for building reliable spreadsheets, tools, and systems. 
It’s not a company or a persona — it’s the design standard that sits behind everything you ship.

2. MISSION

Turn messy, scattered work into clean, governed systems that:
- make decisions easier,
- make audits cleaner,
- and make your future self’s life simpler.

3. VISION

DDL is the layer that:
- catches chaos before it leaks into your life,
- turns it into repeatable structures,
- and preserves those structures so you can grow instead of constantly rebuilding.

If something is important enough to track, DDL’s job is to:
- model it,
- govern it,
- automate it,
- and make it look good.

4. CORE TENETS (DDL Build Sequence)

1) Gather chaos  
   - Collect raw inputs: files, emails, exports, screenshots, notes.  
   - No judgment, just capture.

2) Sort it  
   - Group by domain, project, and intent.  
   - Separate reference from events from decisions.

3) Structure it  
   - Define fact tables, dimension tables, and admin layers.  
   - Decide the grain, keys, and relationships.

4) Encode it  
   - Turn rules into formulas, tables, and validation.  
   - Make logic explicit instead of “remembered in your head.”

5) Govern it  
   - Add standards, protocols, and guardrails.  
   - Protect data integrity and prevent silent drift.

6) Automate it  
   - Use formulas, spill ranges, and macros to reduce manual work.  
   - Design for “click once, update everywhere.”

7) Beautify it  
   - Apply consistent formatting, layouts, and branding.  
   - Make tools pleasant enough that people actually use them.

8) Preserve it  
   - Archive versions, log changes, and capture decisions.  
   - Make it easy to restart after a break or hand off to someone else.

5. SCOPE

DDL applies to:
- Excel workbooks and data models
- Audit tools and trackers
- DexOS-aligned projects that need structure and governance

It does NOT:
- create fictional worlds
- manage emotional processing
- replace DexOS governance (it plugs into it)

6. INTERFACE WITH DEXOS

- DexOS defines behavior, modes, and governance on the AI side.  
- DDL defines structure, standards, and file architecture on the spreadsheet side.  

Together:
- DexOS thinks clearly.
- DDL builds clearly.

7. VERSIONING

This is DDL_CoreCharter_v1.0.
Future versions should track:
- updates to tenets,
- expanded scope,
- changes to naming conventions.

sections.title: Standards Index
sections.preview: Centralized index of all DDL standards, protocols, rules, and specifications with version tracking.
sections.content:
FILE: DDL_Standards_Index_v1.0.txt
TITLE: Dropdown Logistics — Standards Index
VERSION: v1.0
STATUS: Active

PURPOSE:
This file provides a centralized index of all DDL standards, protocols, rules, and specifications.
Each entry links to its file location, purpose, and version history. 
The index is designed to expand over time as DDL grows.

------------------------------------------------------------
1. ACTIVE STANDARDS (Expandable Table)
------------------------------------------------------------

| Code | Standard Name                     | File Location                  | Purpose                                       | Version | Status  |
|------|-----------------------------------|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|---------|---------|
| STD-00 | Blank Standard Protocol          | 02_Protocols                    | Base template for any new protocol.           | v1.0    | Active  |
| STD-01 | Dynamic Dimension Protocol       | 02_Protocols                    | DIM + FACT architecture rules.                | v1.0    | Active  |
| STD-02 | DDL Project Boot Protocol        | 02_Protocols                    | Standard for spinning up new DDL projects.    | v1.0    | Active  |
| STD-03 | DDL Core Charter                 | 01_Admin                        | Vision, mission, and tenets for DDL.          | v1.0    | Active  |

(Add new rows as DDL expands.)

------------------------------------------------------------
2. STANDARDS IN DEVELOPMENT
------------------------------------------------------------

| Code | Standard Name           | Owner | Notes | Expected Version |
|------|--------------------------|--------|-------|-------------------|
| TBD  | Timesheet Standard       | Dave   | Formalize workbook model. | v1.0 |
| TBD  | Assurance Map Standard   | Dave   | Data model + analytics rules. | v1.0 |
| TBD  | Branding Application Std | Dave   | How to apply logos/colors systematically. | v1.0 |

------------------------------------------------------------
3. ARCHIVED / RETIRED STANDARDS
------------------------------------------------------------
(Empty — future use)

------------------------------------------------------------
4. VERSION HISTORY
------------------------------------------------------------

v1.0 — 2025-12-05  
• Created initial index structure  
• Added four active standards  
• Added expandable tables for future standards

sections.content:
FILE: DDL_BootPrompts_Index_v1.0.txt
TITLE: Dropdown Logistics — Boot Prompt Index
VERSION: v1.0

PURPOSE:
This index stores all official boot prompts for launching DDL-affiliated projects or threads.
Boot prompts define how Dex should initialize itself, interpret the project, and collaborate.

------------------------------------------------------------
1. ACTIVE BOOT PROMPTS
------------------------------------------------------------

| Code | Prompt Name                      | Purpose                                     | Version | Notes |
|------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|---------|-------|
| BP-00 | DDL Project Boot Loader          | Standard Dex boot for any DDL project.       | v1.0    | Great for Timesheet, Assurance, Rent, etc. |
| BP-01 | Assurance Map Project Boot       | Specialized boot for AssuranceMap_v1.0.      | v1.0    | Loaded with dataset context. |
| BP-02 | CollabOps v2.1 Work Thread Boot  | Boot for general real-world work threads.    | v2.1    | Lightweight collaborator mode. |

(Add new rows as new projects are launched.)

------------------------------------------------------------
2. TEMPLATE STORAGE (Optional)
------------------------------------------------------------

This folder should store:
- Blank boot forms
- Dex-Ready boot loaders
- Project-specific variants

Recommended layout:
- /Templates/DDL_BootLoader_Template.txt  
- /Templates/DDL_ProjectBoot_Form.txt

------------------------------------------------------------
3. ARCHIVED / SUPERSEDED BOOT PROMPTS
------------------------------------------------------------
(Empty for now)

------------------------------------------------------------
4. VERSION HISTORY
------------------------------------------------------------

v1.0 — 2025-12-05  
• Created Boot Prompt Index  
• Added three active boot prompts  
• Added template storage guidance

sections.title: Changelog Template
sections.preview: Consistent, lightweight changelog format for any DDL project. Tracks what changed, why, and system impact.
sections.content:
FILE: DDL_Project_Changelog_Template_v1.0.txt
TITLE: Dropdown Logistics — Project Changelog Template
VERSION: v1.0

PURPOSE:
This template provides a consistent, lightweight changelog format for any DDL project.
It tracks what changed, why it changed, and how it affects the system.

------------------------------------------------------------
PROJECT NAME:
(Enter name here — Timesheet, Assurance Map, etc.)

VERSIONING RULES:
• Increment MINOR version for logic changes or new tabs  
• Increment MAJOR version for architectural changes  
• Patch version optional for cosmetic or small fixes  

Format: v[Major].[Minor] — YYYY-MM-DD

------------------------------------------------------------
CHANGELOG ENTRIES (Expandable)
------------------------------------------------------------

v1.0 — YYYY-MM-DD
INITIAL RELEASE
• Project created  
• Baseline admin/backend/entry structure established  
• Added: (list)  
• Notes:  
    - Why this version exists  
    - Outstanding tasks  

v1.1 — YYYY-MM-DD
• (Describe feature added)  
• (Describe logic improvement or fix)  
• (Describe tab additions or architectural change)  
• Notes: impact analysis (if needed)

v1.2 — YYYY-MM-DD
• (Continue pattern)

(Add future versions as needed.)

------------------------------------------------------------
HOW TO USE THIS TEMPLATE:
1. Copy this file into the project’s Admin folder as:  
   Project_Changelog_v1.0.txt  
2. Update the version at the top.  
3. Add entries each time a significant change is made.  
4. Archive older versions in the /00_Archive folder.

------------------------------------------------------------
VERSION HISTORY
------------------------------------------------------------

v1.0 — 2025-12-05  
• Created master changelog template for all DDL projects

CHANGELOG:
v1.0 — Initial template created.TITLE: Dropdown Logistics – Governance Model v1.0  
STATUS: Live  
LAST UPDATED: 2025-12-05  
OWNER: Dave  

PURPOSE  
Define how Dropdown Logistics structures decisions, versions, updates, and file status.  
This is not corporate bureaucracy — it’s clarity, speed, and consistency.

----------------------------------------------------------------------
1. DECISION TIERS
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Tier 1 – Creative Direction  
• Branding  
• Vision, Mission, Tenets  
• Naming  
• Architectural direction  
Owner: Dave (Dex advises)

Tier 2 – System Architecture & Standards  
• Protocols  
• Logic standards  
• Naming conventions  
• Documentation rules  
Co-Owners: Dave + Dex

Tier 3 – Project Implementation  
• Workbook builds  
• Automation  
• Design assets  
• Data models  
Owner: Dave (Dex builds with/for)

----------------------------------------------------------------------
2. VERSION STATUS DEFINITIONS
----------------------------------------------------------------------
DRAFT  
• Working copy  
• Can be messy  
• Not yet ready for use  

LIVE  
• Default version used in operations  
• Clean, correct, stable  

FROZEN  
• Protected version (no edits)  
• Used to preserve major milestones or eras  

ARCHIVED  
• Deprecated but kept for reference  
• Moved to /00_Archive when replaced

----------------------------------------------------------------------
3. CHANGE PROCESS
----------------------------------------------------------------------
• Minor edits: allowed instantly  
• Structural edits: require version bump  
• Major overhauls: create a new file (v2.0, v3.0)  
• Dex always proposes simplifications if overbuild starts creeping in

----------------------------------------------------------------------
4. GOVERNANCE PRINCIPLES
----------------------------------------------------------------------
• Keep it simple  
• No unnecessary ceremony  
• Clear over perfect  
• Forward motion > delayed perfection  
• Everything traceable  
• Everything reproducible  
• Files must explain themselves

sections.title: Vision, Mission & Values
sections.preview: Create systems that turn chaos into clarity — beautifully. Six values, eight tenets, one brand statement.
sections.content:
11_DDL_VisionMissionValues_v1.0

TITLE: Dropdown Logistics – Vision, Mission, Values v1.0  
STATUS: Live  

VISION  
Create systems that turn chaos into clarity — beautifully.

MISSION  
Build tools, workflows, and architectures that:  
• simplify complexity  
• reduce friction  
• elevate quality  
• automate the boring  
• make humans smarter, faster, and more confident  

VALUES  
1. Precision  
   – We build things that work and keep working.  
2. Clarity  
   – If someone has to guess, we failed.  
3. Momentum  
   – Keep moving, keep improving. Iterate fast.  
4. Craft  
   – Even utility tools deserve beauty.  
5. Transparency  
   – Every choice visible. Every change traceable.  
6. Humanity  
   – Tech should feel like a partner, not a burden.

CORE TENETS (THE EIGHT)  
1. Gather chaos  
2. Sort it  
3. Structure it  
4. Encode it  
5. Govern it  
6. Automate it  
7. Beautify it  
8. Preserve it  

BRAND STATEMENT  
Dropdown Logistics builds modern, elegant systems  
that make work feel lighter and brains feel sharper.

sections.title: Glossary
sections.preview: Shared language across all DDL projects. From Assurance Mapping Engine to Dynamic Dimensions.
sections.content:
TITLE: Dropdown Logistics – Organizational Glossary v1.0  
STATUS: Live  

PURPOSE  
Define shared language across all DDL projects.

----------------------------------------------------------------------
A–D
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Assurance Mapping Engine  
– Project for unifying 1LOD/2LOD/3LOD assurance data.

Boot Protocol  
– A structured starting packet given to Dex or other LLMs.

CollabOps  
– The way Dave and Dex collaborate: grounded, efficient, human.

DexOS  
– The architecture used for reasoning, governance, and clarity.

DexHub  
– The file system + project hub for Dex work.

Dynamic Dimension  
– A modular system design principle that adapts to user perspective.  
(This underpins scalable Excel builds.)

Dropdown Logistics (DDL)  
– The umbrella org for all tools, designs, workflows, and systems.

----------------------------------------------------------------------
E–P
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Freeze  
– File version locked for posterity; cannot be changed.

Live Version  
– Current canonical version.

Logic Standards  
– Rules that ensure workbook formulas are structured and stable.

Metadata  
– High-level context about scope, purpose, and structure of any file.

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Q–Z
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Protocol  
– A standardized operating document for building or governing.

Roadmap  
– The high-level view of current + future projects.

Session Log  
– Record of each work session for fast re-entry next time.

Tenets  
– The eight guiding principles of DDL (see Vision/Mission Values).

sections.title: Branding Standards
sections.preview: Visual identity system: logo marks, color palette, typography, layout principles, and usage rules.
sections.content:
FILE: 00_BrandingStandards_v1.0.txt
TITLE: Dropdown Logistics — Branding Standards v1.0
STATUS: Active
OWNER: Dave (Creative Director, Systems Architect)
LAST UPDATED: 2025-12-05

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1. BRAND ESSENCE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Dropdown Logistics (DDL) exists at the intersection of:
• Systems thinking  
• Elegant structure  
• Technical clarity  
• Visual confidence  

The brand identity must reflect:
• precision  
• modern engineering  
• thoughtful organization  
• quiet authority  
• “structured calm”  

DDL’s visual language should reinforce the core tenets:
1. Gather chaos  
2. Sort it  
3. Structure it  
4. Encode it  
5. Govern it  
6. Automate it  
7. Beautify it  
8. Preserve it  

Every visual output should feel like step 7: **beautified structure.**

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2. CORE LOGO SYSTEM
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

DDL currently supports two official marks:

A) **The Dropdown Selector Mark**
   Description: A graphical dropdown menu with “Drop / Down / Logistics” stacked.
   Usage:
     • light or conversational brand moments  
     • educational materials  
     • product/UI-inspired design  
   Personality: Friendly, smart, instantly recognizable.

B) **The Circuit-Crest Monogram**
   Description: Stylized “DD” or “DDL” wrapped in circuit-style radiating lines.
   Usage:
     • formal branding  
     • product packaging  
     • templates and documents  
     • engineering-forward materials  
   Personality: Technical, authoritative, system-engineered.

RULES:
• Never stretch, distort, or recolor arbitrarily.  
• Maintain clear space equal to at least the height of the D’s vertical stroke.  
• Do not place over busy textures unless opacity or mask is applied.  

Approved Logo Files:
• 02_LogoExample1.png  
• 03_LogoExample2.png  
• 04_LogoExample3.png  
• 05_LogoExample4.png  
• 06_LogoExample5.png  
• 07_LogoExample6.png  

(Place all canonical files in /03_Branding.)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3. COLOR SYSTEM — v1.0
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PRIMARY PALETTE  
• Deep Navy — #0D1B2A  
  Purpose: Foundation, structure, authority, major backgrounds.

SECONDARY PALETTE  
• Crimson Red — #9B111E  
  Purpose: Action, energy, focus, buttons, highlights.

NEUTRAL PALETTE  
• Neutral Base — #705D50  
• Neutral Base (Slate) — #2C2C2C  
• Neutral Base (Charcoal) — #8B8B05  
Purpose: Surfaces, containers, alternate backgrounds, Excel-like neutrals.

SIGNALS  
• Positive Signal (Deep Green) — #0E6C25  
  Purpose: Success, positive states.  
• Negative Signal (Muted Red) — #F4C6C6  
  Purpose: Errors, voids, corrective states.  
• Highlight (Excel Yellow) — #F7E7A9  
  Purpose: New content, informational notes, callouts.  

ACCENTS  
• Accent — #1B263B  
• Disabled/Muted — #5C677D  

BRAND PRINCIPLE:  
*The palette should feel engineered, confident, and functional — not playful.*

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4. TYPOGRAPHY SYSTEM
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRIMARY TYPEFACE  
• **Inter** or comparable geometric sans-serif  
  Purpose: modern, readable, versatile.

SECONDARY TYPEFACE  
• **Roboto Mono** or any monospaced alternative  
  Purpose: code blocks, data tables, system labels.

RULES:
• No serif fonts anywhere in DDL brand materials.  
• Maintain consistent line height (1.15–1.25).  
• Always use monospaced fonts for formulas, code, and macros.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5. LAYOUT PRINCIPLES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DDL layouts must emphasize:
• clarity  
• modularity  
• structured whitespace  
• high signal-to-noise  

Grid Rules:
• 8-point or 12-point spacing grid  
• Align every element to grid edges  
• Avoid visual clutter or unnecessary ornamentation  

Callout Blocks:
• Use Highlight Yellow only when drawing attention  
• Avoid overuse  

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6. ICONOGRAPHY
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Style:
• clean line icons  
• technical, minimal, geometric  
• consistent stroke width  

Color:
• Navy or Accent as default  
• Red for active or alert states  
• Green only for success/positive indicators  

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7. FILE STRUCTURE (OFFICIAL)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

/03_Branding  
    00_Archive/  
    01_ColorPalette.png  
    02_LogoExample1.png  
    03_LogoExample2.png  
    04_LogoExample3.png  
    05_LogoExample4.png  
    06_LogoExample5.png  
    07_LogoExample6.png  
    00_BrandingStandards_v1.0.txt  

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8. USAGE RULES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Approved Uses:
• reports  
• Excel tools  
• workbooks  
• DexOS-adjacent documentation  
• technical design documents  
• branding assets  

Not Approved:
• memes (unless branded intentionally)  
• fluorescent or pastel recolors  
• cartoonish proportions  
• gradients unrelated to color strategy  

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9. EXPANSION ROADMAP (v1.x → v2.0)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
v1.1 — Add typography examples and spacing templates  
v1.2 — Add branded table formats (Excel + PDF)  
v1.3 — Add PowerPoint / dashboard kits  
v1.4 — Add DexOS integration guidelines  
v2.0 — Release full DDL Brand Guide with templates  

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
10. CHANGELOG
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
v1.0 — Initial release  
       • Defined logo set  
       • Established color palette  
       • Added layout + typography guidelines  
       • Defined file structure  
       • Added usage rules  
       • Created roadmap

sections.title: Generator Tools
sections.preview: README generator, session log writer, changelog builder, proposal bootloader, and metadata auto-builder.
sections.content:
FILE: 01_README_Generator_v2.0.txt
TITLE: DDL README Generator — Advanced
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Convert rough project notes into a clean, DDL-style README that can live at the
  root of any project folder. Supports technical + non-technical projects, and
  can output in TXT or Markdown.

CAPABILITIES
  • Accepts messy notes, bullets, or stream-of-consciousness
  • Extracts project purpose, scope, status, files, and usage
  • Distinguishes between “now” and “future” work
  • Can generate different flavors: dev-facing vs exec-facing
  • Can emit plain text or Markdown, on request

RAW INPUT SECTION (user fills ABOVE this line)
------------------------------------------------------------
<< RAW PROJECT NOTES GO HERE >>
------------------------------------------------------------

USAGE
  1) Paste your raw notes above this file.
  2) Paste or upload this tool below them.
  3) Say: “Generate a README for this project” or similar.
  4) Optionally specify:
     • “Make it Markdown”
     • “Keep it developer-facing”
     • “Make it executive-friendly”

OUTPUT TEMPLATE (BASELINE)
------------------------------------------------------------
README TITLE:
<Short, clear project name>

SUMMARY:
<2–4 sentences: what this is and why it exists>

WHAT THIS PROJECT DOES:
• <Core function 1>
• <Core function 2>
• <Core function 3>

SCOPE:
• In scope:
  – <Item>
  – <Item>
• Out of scope:
  – <Item>
  – <Item>

FILES & STRUCTURE:
• <Folder or key file> — <what it contains>
• <Folder or key file> — <what it contains>

HOW TO USE:
1) <Step>
2) <Step>
3) <Step>

CURRENT STATUS:
• Stage: [Idea | In Progress | Pilot | Live | Maintenance]
• Last meaningful update:
• Known gaps / limitations:

FUTURE IMPROVEMENTS:
• <Planned or possible enhancement>
• <Planned or possible enhancement>

VERSION HISTORY:
• vX.X — <Short description>

------------------------------------------------------------
RULES
  • Use the user’s language where clear; normalize only when needed.
  • Prefer brevity over verbosity; the README should be scannable.
  • If parts of the template don’t apply, omit them rather than forcing filler.
  • If crucial info is missing (e.g., “how to use”), infer only what is safe and
    mark anything important as: “TODO: User to define.”
  • No fictional systems, personas, or lore. This is real-world documentation.

ADVANCED OPTIONS (IF REQUESTED)
  • “Create a dev README and an exec summary version.”
  • “Generate a short ‘Elevator Pitch’ at the top.”
  • “Emit this as Markdown with proper headings.”
  • “Add a ‘Risks & Dependencies’ section.”

CHANGELOG
  v1.0 — Initial README generator.
  v2.0 — Added multi-flavor output, exec/dev split, and better structure for DDL.

FILE: 01_SessionLog_AutoWriter_v2.0.txt
TITLE: DDL Session Log Auto-Writer
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Turn a working session (chat transcript, notes, or memory) into a structured
  session log entry that captures what happened, why it mattered, and how to pick
  back up next time — without having to reread the whole conversation.

CAPABILITIES
  • Summarizes actions taken and decisions made
  • Captures reasoning / intent, not just steps
  • Records risks, open questions, and pending tasks
  • Generates a “re-entry prompt” (DexPrompt) for next time
  • Can produce logs per session or per significant sub-block

RAW INPUT SECTION (user fills ABOVE this line)
------------------------------------------------------------
<< SESSION NOTES / TRANSCRIPT EXCERPTS GO HERE >>
------------------------------------------------------------

USAGE
  1) Paste relevant session content above (can be full or partial).
  2) Paste this tool below.
  3) Say: “Generate a session log,” or:
     • “Create a concise session log”
     • “Focus on decisions and next steps”

BASE SESSION LOG TEMPLATE
------------------------------------------------------------
SESSION ID:
<YYYY-MM-DD + short label>

DATE:
<MM/DD/YYYY>

DURATION (ESTIMATED):
<If inferable, else “Not specified”>

CONTEXT:
• <1–3 bullets describing what this session was about>

ACTIONS TAKEN:
• <Specific actions or artifacts created>
• <Code, files, protocols, specs, or decisions>

KEY DECISIONS:
• <Decision + short rationale>
• <Decision + short rationale>

REASONING / INSIGHTS:
• <What we learned, patterns we saw, clarified thinking>

RISKS / CONCERNS:
• <Technical, cognitive, organizational, or time risks flagged>

OPEN QUESTIONS:
• <Unresolved items that need future attention>

NEXT ACTIONS:
• <1–5 concrete next steps>
• <Include owner or “me” if obvious>

DEXPROMPT (RE-ENTRY PROMPT FOR NEXT SESSION):
A short prompt the user can paste next time to reboot context quickly, e.g.:

“Last session we did X, decided Y, and left Z open. Today, help me:
  1) …
  2) …
  3) …”

------------------------------------------------------------
RULES
  • Prioritize things future-you would actually care about.
  • Don’t restate every detail — compress to signal.
  • Maintain a neutral but human tone; this is a log, not a sales pitch.
  • If input spans multiple mini-sessions, either:
    – consolidate into one log, OR
    – generate multiple logs and clearly label them.

ADVANCED OPTIONS
  • “Generate just the DexPrompt plus bullets of what we did.”
  • “Output a very short log (max 10 lines).”
  • “Tag this log with related projects or files.”

CHANGELOG
  v1.0 — Initial session log helper.
  v2.0 — Added DexPrompt, risk/open question capture, and multi-session handling.

FILE: 02_Changelog_Generator_v2.0.txt
TITLE: DDL Changelog Generator — Advanced Edition
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Transform raw notes, session logs, metadata, or freeform text into a structured,
  multi-entry DDL-compliant CHANGELOG. Supports semantic versioning, grouped
  changes, multiple change types, inferred relationships, and summary synthesis.

CAPABILITIES
  • Accepts ANY quantity of raw notes (1 line → 50 pages)
  • Auto-detects version numbers or assigns one
  • Groups related changes under: Features, Improvements, Fixes, Refactors, Docs
  • Distills messy notes into crisp, meaningful statements
  • Highlights impact areas and downstream dependencies
  • Produces single or multiple changelog entries depending on input
  • Provides warnings when notes are unclear or contradictory
  • Maintains DDL tone: direct, crisp, structured

RAW INPUT SECTION (User fills above this file)
------------------------------------------------------------
<< RAW INPUT WILL BE PASTED ABOVE THIS LINE >>

USAGE
  After pasting raw input at the top of the file:
  → Paste or summon this tool
  → Say: “Generate changelog” or “Run changelog generator”

OUTPUT TEMPLATE
------------------------------------------------------------
VERSION: <vX.X.X or inferred>
DATE: <MM/DD/YYYY or inferred>
ENTRIES:

[FEATURES]
• <Feature-level changes, grouped and cleaned>

[IMPROVEMENTS]
• <Enhancements, optimizations, UX polish>

[FIXES]
• <Bug fixes, error corrections, resolved issues>

[REFACTORS]
• <Restructuring, simplifications, cleanup>

[DOCUMENTATION]
• <README, protocols, templates, standards updates>

[OTHER]
• <Notes that do not fit categories but matter>

IMPACTS:
• <Systems affected>
• <Files modified>
• <Upstream or downstream implications>
• <Migration or update considerations>

NOTES:
• Optional contextual commentary if valuable.

------------------------------------------------------------
RULES FOR INTERPRETATION
  1. If version missing → infer from context or mark as “Unspecified”.
  2. If entries are chronological → detect and separate.
  3. If user mixes several categories → auto-group by meaning.
  4. If user includes emotional chatter → strip respectfully but preserve intent.
  5. If contradictions exist → resolve gently and note uncertainty.
  6. Do not fabricate features or pretend knowledge not present.

ADVANCED OPTIONS (User may request)
  • “Split entries by date”
  • “Summarize only features”
  • “Generate executive-summary version”
  • “Generate release-notes version”
  • “Convert to markdown”
  • “Convert to JSON for automation”
  • “Generate cumulative changelog”

------------------------------------------------------------
CHANGELOG HISTORY
v1.0 — Basic generator created.  
v2.0 — Multi-entry, multi-category, auto-versioning, impact analysis, and advanced
        formatting modes added.

FILE: 03_Proposal_Bootloader_v2.0.txt
TITLE: DDL Proposal Bootloader
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Take a rough project idea or request and turn it into a structured DDL project
  proposal, aligned with the 00_DDL_Proposal_Template_v1.0 (or later). This is
  what you use when a “hey, I had an idea…” moment needs to become a real, tracked
  initiative.

CAPABILITIES
  • Converts messy idea descriptions into clean proposals
  • Separates motivation, scope, risks, and deliverables
  • Distinguishes MVP vs. future phases
  • Flags alignment with DDL tenets when possible
  • Can generate both a short and long version if requested

RAW INPUT SECTION (user fills ABOVE this line)
------------------------------------------------------------
<< RAW PROJECT IDEA / REQUEST GOES HERE >>
------------------------------------------------------------

USAGE
  1) Paste idea / context above.
  2) Paste this tool definition below it.
  3) Say: “Generate a DDL proposal” or “Turn this into a proposal.”

BASE OUTPUT TEMPLATE (aligned with DDL proposal style)
------------------------------------------------------------
PROJECT TITLE:
<Short, descriptive name>

VERSION:
v1.0 (initial proposal)

OWNER:
<Who is responsible / lead architect>

SUMMARY:
<3–5 sentence overview of what this is and why it exists>

PROBLEM / OPPORTUNITY:
• <What pain or gap this addresses>
• <Why now>

OBJECTIVES:
• <Goal 1>
• <Goal 2>
• <Goal 3>

SCOPE (PHASE 1 / MVP):
• In scope:
  – <Item>
  – <Item>
• Out of scope (future phase or explicitly excluded):
  – <Item>
  – <Item>

DELIVERABLES:
• <Concrete artifact or outcome 1>
• <Concrete artifact or outcome 2>

STAKEHOLDERS:
• <Primary users / beneficiaries>
• <Dependencies on other people/systems>

ALIGNMENT WITH DDL / DEX PRINCIPLES:
• <How this supports clarity, structure, momentum, or tooling>

RISKS & CONSTRAINTS:
• <Technical, time, cognitive, or adoption risks>
• <Known constraints>

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
• <What “done” looks like in practice>

NEXT ACTIONS:
• <1–3 concrete steps to move this from idea → active project>

------------------------------------------------------------
RULES
  • Do not over-promise; proposals should be realistic, not aspirational fiction.
  • If any section is unclear from input, fill with “TODO: Clarify…” rather than
    guessing wildly.
  • Use the user’s own language where it carries intent; normalize only for
    clarity and structure.
  • Keep one proposal per run; if multiple ideas exist, either:
    – generate multiple proposals, or
    – explicitly recommend splitting.

ADVANCED OPTIONS
  • “Generate an Exec Lite proposal version (1-page).”
  • “Generate a Technical Appendix describing systems and data.”
  • “Highlight overlap with existing active projects.”

CHANGELOG
  v1.0 — Initial proposal bootloader.
  v2.0 — DDL-aligned, with explicit scope, criteria, and alignment sections.

FILE: 04_Metadata_AutoBuilder_v2.0.txt
TITLE: DDL Metadata Auto-Builder
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Build rich, structured metadata blocks for projects, files, or workbooks:
  ownership, purpose, dependencies, interfaces, and tags. This is the glue for
  discoverability and governance across DDL.

CAPABILITIES
  • Handles single file, whole project, or workbook description
  • Extracts or infers: purpose, owner, status, dependencies, interfaces
  • Can output per-file metadata and/or project-level metadata
  • Can emit JSON or YAML-style blocks if requested

RAW INPUT SECTION (user fills ABOVE this line)
------------------------------------------------------------
<< DESCRIPTION OF PROJECT/FILES/WORKBOOK GOES HERE >>
------------------------------------------------------------

USAGE
  1) Paste description above (could be README, notes, or just a paragraph).
  2) Paste this file after it.
  3) Say: “Generate metadata for this project” or “Create file-level metadata.”

BASE OUTPUT TEMPLATE — PROJECT METADATA
------------------------------------------------------------
PROJECT_NAME:
VERSION:
OWNER:
DESCRIPTION:
STATUS: [Idea | Active | Paused | Deprecated | Complete]
PRIMARY_LOCATION: <Folder path or system>

FILES:
• <FileName.ext> — <Purpose / role>
• <FileName.ext> — <Purpose / role>

DEPENDENCIES:
• Systems:
  – <System / tool / integration>
• Data Sources:
  – <Source or dataset>
• People:
  – <If relevant>

INTERFACES:
• Inputs:
  – <What comes in, from where>
• Outputs:
  – <What comes out, where it goes>

RISK_LEVEL: [Low | Medium | High]
SECURITY_OR_SENSITIVITY:
• <PII/PHI/Confidential/Restricted/None>

TAGS:
• <Keyword 1>
• <Keyword 2>
• <Domain tags: e.g., Excel, Audit, DexOS, etc.>

LAST_UPDATED:
<MM/DD/YYYY or “Not specified”>

------------------------------------------------------------
OPTIONAL PER-FILE METADATA TEMPLATE
(Use when requested: “Also create per-file metadata.”)
------------------------------------------------------------

FILE: 05_SessionLog_AutoWriter_v2.0.txt
TITLE: DDL Session Log Auto-Writer
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Turn a working session (chat transcript, notes, or memory) into a structured
  session log entry that captures what happened, why it mattered, and how to pick

  • Captures reasoning / intent, not just steps
  • Records risks, open questions, and pending tasks

  • Can produce logs per session or per significant sub-block

  2) Paste this tool below.

BASE SESSION LOG TEMPLATE
------------------------------------------------------------
SESSION ID:
<YYYY-MM-DD + short label>

DATE:
<MM/DD/YYYY>

DURATION (ESTIMATED):
<If inferable, else “Not specified”>

CONTEXT:

ACTIONS TAKEN:

• <Code, files, protocols, specs, or decisions>

KEY DECISIONS:
• <Decision + short rationale>
• <Decision + short rationale>

REASONING / INSIGHTS:

RISKS / CONCERNS:

OPEN QUESTIONS:

NEXT ACTIONS:

DEXPROMPT (RE-ENTRY PROMPT FOR NEXT SESSION):

“Last session we did X, decided Y, and left Z open. Today, help me:

  3) …”

------------------------------------------------------------

  • Maintain a neutral but human tone; this is a log, not a sales pitch.
  • If input spans multiple mini-sessions, either:
    – consolidate into one log, OR
    – generate multiple logs and clearly label them.

sections.title: Validator Tools
sections.preview: Naming convention validator, file structure validator, data governance checker, and workbook logic inspector.
sections.content:
FILE: README_Validators_v2.0.txt
TITLE: DDL Validators Suite — Overview & Usage
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  This README provides a clear, quick-reference overview for the Validators
  suite under: 05_Tools / 02_Validators.

  Validators check STRUCTURE, NAMING, DATA QUALITY, and LOGIC HEALTH across
  Dropdown Logistics projects. They prevent drift, inconsistency, and silent
  technical debt.

WHEN TO USE VALIDATORS
  Use this suite whenever:
    • a project grows in complexity
    • naming starts to feel inconsistent
    • folders begin accumulating versions
    • datasets or fields feel messy or unclear
    • Excel logic is getting fragile
    • you’re preparing to ship or archive a project
    • onboarding someone into a project

FOLDER CONTENTS (v2.0)
  01_NamingConvention_Validator_v2.0.txt
      Checks filenames, sheet names, table names, field names
      Flags inconsistent patterns and suggests corrected versions.

  02_FileStructure_Validator_v2.0.txt
      Validates project folder hierarchy against DDL standards.
      Produces corrected folder trees and risk notes.

  03_DataGovernance_Checker_v2.0.txt
      Examines datasets (CSV/Excel) for naming, keys, normalization,
      sensitivity classification, and governance practices.

  04_WorkbookLogic_Inspector_v2.0.txt
      Analyzes formulas, named ranges, and dependencies in Excel.
      Flags brittle logic and recommends refactors.

COMMON WORKFLOW
  1. Copy the validator file you want to use into your workspace.
  2. Paste your raw input where indicated (filenames, folder tree, fields, formulas).
  3. Ask Dex: “Run validation.”
  4. Review flagged issues + recommended refactors.
  5. Apply changes in your project.
  6. Optionally run the validator again to confirm compliance.

OUTPUT CONSISTENCY
  All validators share a common output philosophy:
    • concise summaries
    • structured findings
    • suggested improvements
    • optional advanced modes (strict, sorted, critical-only)
    • versioned naming for traceability

VERSIONING RULES
  v1.x — prototype or simple checks  
  v2.x — full suite with structured outputs and improvement engine  
  v3.x — integrated & automated utilities (future)

RELATIONSHIP TO OTHER TOOL FOLDERS
  /01_Generators = Create new documentation & scaffolds  
  /02_Validators = Check quality, consistency, and structure  
  /03_Transformers = Convert data between formats  
  /00_Archive = Store deprecated tools & old versions  

NOTES
  • Validators DO NOT modify files directly.
  • They provide human-readable analysis and recommended changes.
  • They align to DDL philosophy: clarity, structure, predictability.

CHANGELOG
  v1.0 — Initial suite created.
  v2.0 — Standardized format, improved validation patterns, added README.

FILE: 01_NamingConvention_Validator_v2.0.txt
TITLE: DDL Naming Convention Validator
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Validate a folder of filenames, workbook tabs, or Excel table/field names
  against DDL naming standards. This tool provides fast, structured feedback
  on clarity, consistency, and alignment with organizational patterns.

CAPABILITIES
  • Accepts lists of filenames, sheet names, table names, or field names
  • Flags violations of DDL naming best practices
  • Suggests corrected names (predictably formatted)
  • Detects pattern drift across large project directories
  • Supports human-friendly mode AND strict mode

RAW INPUT (user fills above this line)
------------------------------------------------------------
<< PASTE FILENAMES / SHEET NAMES / TABLE NAMES HERE >>
------------------------------------------------------------

USAGE
  1) Paste your list above.
  2) Provide this tool beneath it.
  3) Say: “Validate naming” or “Show corrected names.”

DDL NAMING RULES ENFORCED
------------------------------------------------------------
• Names must be descriptive, scannable, and specific.
• Use underscores or PascalCase; avoid spaces.
• Include version numbers only at the END (e.g., _v1.0).
• Avoid ambiguous abbreviations unless standardized.
• Keep related items alphabetically sortable.
• NEVER mix different naming conventions within a folder.
• Reserve `00_` prefixes for foundational or boot files.
• Use consistent ordering:
    00_ (root/boot)
    01_ (core)
    02_ (models)
    03_ (protocols)
    etc.

BASE OUTPUT TEMPLATE
------------------------------------------------------------
VALIDATION SUMMARY:
• Total items checked:
• Items passing:
• Items needing revision:

DETAILED FINDINGS:
• <Name> — PASS
• <Name> — Needs revision
     Reason:
     Suggested Fix:

PATTERN ANALYSIS:
• <Patterns detected that help or hinder clarity>

RECOMMENDED REFACTORS:
• <Suggested renames for consistency>

------------------------------------------------------------
ADVANCED OPTIONS
  • “Apply strict mode.”
  • “Sort output alphabetically.”
  • “Generate a batch rename script (PowerShell/Terminal).”
  • “Highlight only the dangerous naming violations.”

CHANGELOG
  v1.0 — Basic filename validator.
  v2.0 — Added Excel field/table support, structured errors, and fix suggestions.

FILE: 02_FileStructure_Validator_v2.0.txt
TITLE: DDL File Structure Validator
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Evaluate whether a project folder tree follows DDL standards for:
    • hierarchy
    • ordering
    • clarity
    • dependency separation
    • versioning
    • archival discipline

CAPABILITIES
  • Accepts a raw folder tree (copied from OS or manually typed)
  • Identifies structural risks or violations
  • Recommends reorganized folder layout
  • Detects version drift, file sprawl, unclear categories
  • Suggests when files should move to /Archive or /Tools

RAW INPUT (user fills above this line)
------------------------------------------------------------
<< PASTE FOLDER TREE HERE >>
------------------------------------------------------------

USAGE
  1) Paste folder structure above.
  2) Include this tool beneath it.
  3) Say: “Validate structure.”

DDL STRUCTURE RULES
------------------------------------------------------------
• Every project must have:
   - 00_Admin/
   - 01_Core/
   - 02_Documents/ or 02_Protocols/
   - 03_Assets/ or Data/
   - 04_Archive/
   - 05_Tools/ (if applicable)
• No file should live at root unless explicitly allowed.
• Versions must live either inside filename or inside /Archive/.
• No duplicate versions scattered across directories.
• Boot files must remain at top-level under 00_Admin.

BASE OUTPUT TEMPLATE
------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY:
• Items checked:
• Structural issues detected:
• Recommended actions:

ISSUES BY CATEGORY:
[STRUCTURE ERRORS]
• <Issue> — <Impact> — <Fix>

[NAMING / VERSION ERRORS]
• <Issue> — <Fix>

[ORGANIZATIONAL RISKS]
• <Issue> — <Fix>

RECOMMENDED RESTRUCTURED TREE:
<Clean, corrected folder map>

NOTES:
• <Any assumptions or uncertainties>

------------------------------------------------------------
ADVANCED OPTIONS
  • “Generate migration plan from old → new structure.”
  • “Highlight archival candidates only.”
  • “Reduce output to critical findings.”

CHANGELOG
  v1.0 — Basic folder validator.
  v2.0 — Added system-wide pattern detection, restructuring engine, and admin folder rules.

FILE: 03_DataGovernance_Checker_v2.0.txt
TITLE: DDL Data Governance Checker
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Examine dataset structures (CSV headers, Excel tables, or raw data schemas)
  to enforce DDL governance principles:
    • clarity
    • field integrity
    • naming consistency
    • normalization
    • version traceability
    • privacy & sensitivity tagging

CAPABILITIES
  • Analyze field names and detect inconsistent patterns
  • Flag unclear identifiers, composite keys, or missing unique constraints
  • Identify redundant fields and normalization issues
  • Detect sensitivity categories (PII, PHI, confidential)
  • Recommend governance tagging and documentation

RAW INPUT (user fills above this line)
------------------------------------------------------------
<< PASTE FIELD LISTS / TABLE SCHEMAS / SAMPLE ROWS HERE >>
------------------------------------------------------------

USAGE
  1) Paste data structure above.
  2) Add this tool definition after it.
  3) Say: “Run data governance check.”

DDL GOVERNANCE RULES
------------------------------------------------------------
• Field names must be readable, consistent, and specific.
• Avoid embedded logic in column names.
• Require predictable key structure (ID, Code, Date, etc.).
• Sensitive fields MUST be flagged with sensitivity levels.
• Dataset must include versioning / update metadata.
• Every dataset needs:
   - Purpose
   - Owner
   - Row count
   - Key structure
   - Dependencies

OUTPUT TEMPLATE
------------------------------------------------------------
GOVERNANCE SUMMARY:
• Risk level:
• Structural issues:
• Recommended cleanups:

FIELD-BY-FIELD ANALYSIS:
<FieldName> — PASS / NEEDS ATTENTION
   Notes:
   Recommended Fix:

KEY STRUCTURE ANALYSIS:
• Suggested primary key:
• Duplication risks:
• Normalization opportunities:

SENSITIVITY FINDINGS:
• <Field> — <Classification>
• <Field> — <Classification>

RECOMMENDATIONS:
• Rename:
• Remove:
• Split:
• Merge:
• Document:

------------------------------------------------------------
ADVANCED OPTIONS
  • “Generate an official Data Dictionary.”
  • “Output a JSON schema.”
  • “Flag fields that violate naming rules.”
  • “Classify fields by business domain.”

CHANGELOG
  v1.0 — Basic field validator.
  v2.0 — Added sensitivity detection, key inference, normalization checks, and data dictionary mode.

FILE: 04_WorkbookLogic_Inspector_v2.0.txt
TITLE: DDL Workbook Logic Inspector
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Analyze formulas, named ranges, table structures, and dependency chains inside
  Excel workbooks (Timesheet, Rent Tracker, Assurance Mapping, etc.). Provides
  early detection of:
    • brittle formulas
    • circular references
    • unnecessary complexity
    • unsafe dependencies
    • broken named ranges
    • hidden logic drift

CAPABILITIES
  • Accepts formula dumps, named range exports, or table schemas
  • Classifies each formula by complexity and fragility
  • Flags INDIRECT, OFFSET, nested IF cascades, and volatile functions
  • Detects redundant calculations and consolidation opportunities
  • Recommends safer design patterns (LET, structured refs, LAMBDA, etc.)
  • Suggests workbook architecture improvements

RAW INPUT (user fills above this line)
------------------------------------------------------------
<< PASTE FORMULAS / NAMED RANGES / TABLE MAPS HERE >>
------------------------------------------------------------

USAGE
  1) Paste relevant workbook pieces above.
  2) Place this tool beneath them.
  3) Say: “Inspect logic” or “Analyze workbook architecture.”

DDL LOGIC RULES
------------------------------------------------------------
• Prefer structured references over A1 references.
• Prefer LET for repeated expressions.
• Avoid INDIRECT unless absolutely necessary.
• Keep volatile functions to a minimum.
• Every formula block must be documented or obvious.
• No hidden dependency chains that aren’t mapped.
• Architecture must prioritize:
   - Stability
   - Traceability
   - Auditability
   - Maintainability

OUTPUT TEMPLATE
------------------------------------------------------------
WORKBOOK SUMMARY:
• Total formulas analyzed:
• High-risk formulas:
• Medium-risk:
• Low-risk:

LOGIC FINDINGS:
[HIGH RISK]
• <Formula> — <Reason> — <Fix>

[MEDIUM RISK]
• <Formula> — <Reason> — <Fix>

[LOW RISK]
• <Formula> — <Notes>

NAMED RANGE ANALYSIS:
• Missing ranges:
• Orphaned ranges:
• Overlapping ranges:
• Recommended cleanup:

DEPENDENCY GRAPH NOTES:
• <Patterns detected>
• <Chains that should be consolidated>

RECOMMENDED REFACTORS:
• <LET opportunities>
• <Table conversions>
• <Simplify nested logic>
• <Break apart overloaded formulas>

------------------------------------------------------------
ADVANCED OPTIONS
  • “Generate a workbook architecture diagram.”
  • “Export issues sorted by sheet.”
  • “Classify formulas by readability score.”
  • “Suggest a migration plan to structured references.”

CHANGELOG
  v1.0 — Initial logic inspector.
  v2.0 — Added risk-classification, dependency mapping, and modernization suggestions.

sections.title: Transformer Tools
sections.preview: CSV-to-model-spec, Excel-to-JSON, field map alignment, and table schema extraction transformers.
sections.content:
FILE: README_Transformers_v2.0.txt
TITLE: DDL Transformers Suite — Overview & Usage
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Transformers convert structures from one form to another:
    • CSV → Model Specs
    • Excel → JSON
    • Field mappings → aligned schemas
    • Tables → structural schema definitions

  These tools reduce translation friction, accelerate onboarding,
  and ensure consistent internal modeling across DDL projects.

WHEN TO USE TRANSFORMERS
  Use this suite when:
    • datasets need to be profiled quickly
    • models or systems require standardized schemas
    • Excel tables must be converted for automation
    • mapping fields across systems
    • preparing clean documentation from messy inputs

FOLDER CONTENTS (v2.0)
  01_CSV_to_Model_Spec_Transformer_v2.0.txt
      Takes a CSV header + sample rows → outputs a model spec
      (fields, types, key candidates, relationships).

  02_Excel_to_JSON_Converter_v2.0.txt
      Converts a table-like Excel range into structured JSON.

  03_FieldMap_AutoAligner_v2.0.txt
      Aligns fields across systems, highlights mismatches,
      recommends unified naming and normalization.

  04_TableSchema_Extractor_v2.0.txt
      Extracts schema (columns, data types, constraints) from Excel tables.

WORKFLOW
  1. Paste the raw input into the tool file where indicated.
  2. Ask Dex: “Run transformer.”
  3. Review transformed output.
  4. Apply or export as needed.

OUTPUT GUARANTEES
  • Consistent formatting  
  • Schema-level clarity  
  • Alignment to DDL naming conventions  
  • Machine-readable structure (JSON, YAML, table)  

RELATIONSHIP TO OTHER FOLDERS
  /01_Generators = produce NEW assets  
  /02_Validators = check EXISTING assets  
  /03_Transformers = convert BETWEEN assets  
  /00_Archive = old or deprecated versions  

CHANGELOG
  v1.0 — Initial tool set.
  v2.0 — Unified format, cleaner output shapes, improved instructions.

FILE: CSV_to_Model_Spec_Transformer_v2.0.txt
TITLE: CSV → Model Spec Transformer
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Convert any CSV file (header + rows) into a clean, structured model spec.

INSTRUCTIONS
  1. Paste your CSV header + 3–10 sample rows below INPUT.
  2. Say “Run transformer.”
  3. Dex outputs:
       • Field names
       • Data type inference
       • Nullability
       • Key candidates
       • Notes on normalization
       • Example Model Spec in JSON + table format

INPUT:
<<PASTE CSV CONTENT HERE>>

OUTPUT FORMAT:
  MODEL_SPEC:
    - field name
    - inferred type
    - nullable?
    - key candidate?
    - notes
  JSON_SCHEMA:
  TABLE_SCHEMA:

NOTES
  For large CSVs, header + small sample is enough for the inference engine.

CHANGELOG
  v1.0 — Basic inference.
  v2.0 — Adds JSON output + key candidate scoring.

FILE: Excel_to_JSON_Converter_v2.0.txt
TITLE: Excel Table → JSON Converter
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Convert an Excel-like table (columns + rows) into structured JSON objects.

INSTRUCTIONS
  1. Paste a table (headers + rows) under INPUT.
  2. Say “Run transformer.”
  3. Dex outputs:
       • JSON array
       • Optional compact JSON
       • Optional indented JSON
       • Field type notes

INPUT:
<<PASTE EXCEL-LIKE TABLE HERE>>

OUTPUT:
  {
    "json_indented": [ ... ],
    "json_compact": "[...]",
    "field_types": { ... }
  }

CHANGELOG
  v1.0 — Basic JSON output.
  v2.0 — Adds type inference + compact/pretty modes.

FILE: FieldMap_AutoAligner_v2.0.txt
TITLE: Field Map Auto-Aligner
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Align two or more field lists across systems and produce:
    • match table
    • mismatched fields
    • naming recommendations
    • normalized unified field list

INSTRUCTIONS
  1. Paste field lists under SOURCE_A and SOURCE_B.
  2. Say “Run alignment.”
  3. Dex outputs:
       • alignment matrix
       • similarity scoring
       • unified model schema
       • rename suggestions

INPUT:
SOURCE_A:
<<LIST>>

SOURCE_B:
<<LIST>>

CHANGELOG
  v1.0 — Basic alignment.
  v2.0 — Adds scoring + unified schema output.

FILE: TableSchema_Extractor_v2.0.txt
TITLE: Table Schema Extractor
VERSION: v2.0

PURPOSE
  Extract structural metadata from an Excel table:
    • column names
    • inferred types
    • constraints
    • uniqueness
    • normalization suggestions

INSTRUCTIONS
  1. Paste table header + 3–5 sample rows.
  2. Say “Extract schema.”
  3. Dex returns:
       • Schema table
       • JSON schema
       • Notes on key candidates
       • Normalization risks

INPUT:
<<PASTE EXCEL TABLE SAMPLE>>

OUTPUT:
  - TABLE_SCHEMA
  - JSON_SCHEMA
  - NORMALIZATION_NOTES

CHANGELOG
  v1.0 — Basic schema extraction.
  v2.0 — Adds normalization notes + JSON schema.
